case_name: Stormwater polishing for uncertain mixed legacy and emerging contaminants before sensitive receiving-water discharge
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for variable stormwater runoff containing mixed VOCs, pesticide, cyanotoxin, and poorly characterized emerging contaminants prior to environmental discharge.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipality is evaluating treatment for urban stormwater captured from a drainage basin receiving highway runoff, light industrial area wash-off, landscaped commercial zones, and episodic inflow from a lake-edge pump station during bloom season. Monitoring has confirmed tetrachloroethylene, MTBE, propoxur, cylindrospermopsin, and lithium, but event-based sampling is sparse and first-flush peaks are poorly defined; additional fuel-related organics and algal metabolites are suspected but not fully characterised. Flow and quality vary sharply between dry-weather base flow, small rain events, and intense storms, creating uncertainty about the sizing and resilience of advanced treatment. The discharge point is a sensitive urban stream with seasonal low dilution, so the city wants a robust but not excessively energy-intensive treatment train.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Treated discharge should reliably reduce suspended solids, pathogen indicators, cyanotoxin risk, pesticides, and volatile/semi-volatile organics to support protective discharge to a sensitive receiving stream, while recognizing that lithium removal may be limited without high-energy desalination.
secondary_goals: maintain performance under first_flush contaminant spikes and wet_weather flow swings
avoid overcommitting to energy-intensive desalination unless clearly justified
provide a barrier for partially characterised emerging contaminants
minimize residuals and spent media handling burdens
explicitly flag uncertainty where contaminant treatability depends on speciation or event timing

core_unit_functions.flow_equalization_and_offline_storage: buffer first_flush peaks and dampen hydraulic variability so downstream units can operate more consistently
core_unit_functions.hydrodynamic_separation_and_sedimentatio: remove grit, settleable solids, and particle-associated contaminant load typical of stormwater
core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation: reduce turbidity, colloids, cyanotoxin-bearing cells or organic matter, and improve filtration and oxidation performance
core_unit_functions.dual_media_filtration: polish suspended solids and lower particle breakthrough before advanced polishing
core_unit_functions.ozonation_or_UV_advanced_oxidation: degrade cylindrospermopsin and help oxidize propoxur and some dissolved organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon: adsorb and biologically polish residual organics including MTBE and some oxidation byproducts while adding resilience for partially characterised contaminants
core_unit_functions.final_UV_disinfection: reduce microbial indicator risk prior to discharge

applicability_rationale: a multi-barrier train is justified because the contaminant set spans particulates, VOC-like compounds, pesticide, cyanotoxin, and uncertain emerging constituents
equalization is especially important for stormwater because first-flush spikes and storm hydrographs can otherwise overwhelm oxidation and adsorption stages
advanced oxidation is well aligned with cyanotoxin control and can provide a hedge against incompletely characterised dissolved organics
BAC after oxidation provides both adsorption and biodegradation capacity, which is useful for MTBE and for polishing oxidation transformation products
this option avoids full desalination, making it more realistic than RO for environmental discharge while still addressing most higher-priority risks
potential_risks: lithium will likely remain only minimally affected; if a numeric lithium discharge limit is later imposed, this train may become insufficient
PCE and MTBE removal through oxidation and BAC is sensitive to dose, contact time, and actual event concentrations, so pilot verification is advisable
ozonation can form bromate or aldehydes if source chemistry is unfavorable, requiring monitoring and optimization
uncertainty remains because some suspected fuel-related compounds and algal metabolites are not fully characterised
performance may deteriorate during very high turbidity storm events if pretreatment and storage are undersized

core_unit_functions.flow_equalization_and_offline_storage: buffer event flow and concentration swings
core_unit_functions.screening_and_sedimentation: remove debris and coarse solids prior to membranes and adsorption
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide strong solids and microbial barrier and protect downstream carbon
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb propoxur, some cylindrospermopsin, and a broad range of hydrophobic or partially known organics
core_unit_functions.air_stripping_or_packed_tower_aeration: remove volatile compounds such as tetrachloroethylene and can also reduce MTBE to a degree depending on design

applicability_rationale: this train separates particulate control from dissolved contaminant control and is practical when oxidation byproduct concerns or ozone complexity are undesirable
air stripping is a recognized option for volatile chlorinated solvents and can complement GAC where VOCs are a concern
UF provides a reliable barrier for variable turbidity stormwater and protects GAC from rapid fouling
GAC offers a broad-spectrum hedge for uncertain organic micropollutants in partially characterised runoff
potential_risks: MTBE is less amenable to air stripping and GAC than many chlorinated solvents, so removal may be inconsistent unless EBCT and air-water ratio are carefully designed
dissolved cylindrospermopsin destruction is less certain than in the oxidation-based option, especially after toxin release from lysed cells
UF fouling risk increases during high-solids storm events unless sedimentation is effective
lithium removal is negligible
off-gas treatment may be needed if stripped VOC emissions become a permitting issue

core_unit_functions.coagulation_flocculation_and_clarificati: remove colloids and lower membrane fouling load
core_unit_functions.ultrafiltration: provide solids barrier and membrane pretreatment
core_unit_functions.nanofiltration_or_low_pressure_reverse_o: remove a broad spectrum of dissolved contaminants and provide the only credible pathway among listed options for meaningful lithium reduction
core_unit_functions.GAC_or_BAC_post_polishing: polish residual organics and improve discharge quality

applicability_rationale: this option is attractive only if regulators or local receiving-water conditions require aggressive control of dissolved micropollutants including lithium
pressure membranes offer broader dissolved contaminant removal than adsorption or oxidation alone
the train is technically robust for uncertainty in unknown dissolved contaminants because it adds a physical separation barrier
potential_risks: brine or concentrate management is a major obstacle and conflicts with stated site constraints
stormwater salinity is only moderate, so desalination may be disproportionate for environmental discharge unless lithium becomes a decisive driver
membrane fouling and variable recovery under storm-driven solids and NOM loads can complicate operations
capital and operator skill requirements are high relative to municipal stormwater programs
recommendation carries a strong uncertainty flag because actual lithium removal need is not yet clearly established by permit language

core_unit_functions.constructed_wetland_or_biofiltration_bas: provide flow attenuation, particulate settling, and partial biological treatment in a nature-based stormwater system
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon: adsorb residual organics and some pesticides or cyanotoxin

applicability_rationale: nature-based pretreatment can be attractive for municipal stormwater where energy use and visible green infrastructure co-benefits matter
the chain can reduce solids and some organic load before discharge with relatively low energy demand
included as a moderate but not top-ranked option because it aligns with stormwater practice yet may not reliably control all target dissolved contaminants
potential_risks: removal of PCE, MTBE, and dissolved cylindrospermopsin is uncertain and event-dependent
seasonal temperature and biological variability can reduce treatment consistency
footprint demand may be difficult at urban sites
sediment contamination and maintenance burden can accumulate over time
lithium control is effectively absent

applicability_rationale: this is a low-complexity baseline for comparison and may improve conventional stormwater quality metrics
it is intentionally ranked low because it does not match the mixed dissolved contaminant challenge in this case
potential_risks: insufficient control of PCE, MTBE, propoxur, cylindrospermopsin, and lithium
chlorination alone is not a reliable barrier for cyanotoxins and may create byproducts in NOM-containing runoff
does not address uncertainty in unknown co-occurring emerging contaminants
likely inadequate for sensitive-stream discharge under low-dilution conditions

preferred_rank_pattern: options that explicitly address both cyanotoxin risk and dissolved organic micropollutants should rank higher
desalination_based_options may appear but should not be ranked first unless lithium control is strongly justified despite brine constraints
nature_based_or_conventional_only_trains should rank lower because they are less reliable for VOCs_and_uncertain_emerging_contaminants

must_reflect: stormwater variability requires buffering_or_offline_storage rather than assuming steady influent
environmental_discharge does not automatically justify high_energy_desalination unless lithium or permit sensitivity clearly drives it
cost_energy_tradeoffs should be discussed when comparing oxidation_adsorption versus membrane_intensive trains
residuals and concentrate handling limitations should materially penalize RO_or_NF_based options
the partially characterised contaminant mixture should favor robust broad_spectrum barriers rather than single_mechanism solutions

must_contain: why equalization is important for first_flush and event driven contamination
why oxidation is relevant for cylindrospermopsin and some micropollutants
why adsorption_or_BAC helps with uncertain residual organics and oxidation byproducts
why lithium is difficult to remove without membrane desalination and why that creates a strategic tradeoff
why simple sedimentation_filtration_disinfection is insufficient for this mixed dissolved contaminant case

should_mention: sparse event_based monitoring creates uncertainty in true peak concentrations and design basis
suspected but not fully characterised co_contaminants may alter treatment selection and monitoring needs
PCE_and_MTBE control may be variable depending on volatility, contact time, and storm event concentration spikes
cyanotoxin risk includes uncertainty over intracellular versus dissolved fractions during bloom season
lithium may remain largely untreated in non_membrane trains and could become a future compliance issue
membrane_options carry fouling and concentrate management risks that may outweigh benefits for environmental discharge
oxidation processes require control of byproducts and demand fluctuations under variable NOM conditions
